title: Distributions
text: These original project notes are influenced by public NC State statistics teaching resources: short conceptual framing, visible math, reproducible R/Quarto code, and simulation/diagnostic thinking.

title: Distributions
text:
1 Purpose
Each variable has an explicit statistical shape. Soccer data mixes low-count outcomes, ordered categorical results, probabilities, text/news counts, player availability flags, and continuous context variables.
The workflow lists plausible distributions, builds diagnostics, and filters models that do not fit the data or the betting question.

title: Distributions
text:
2 Current Outcome Shapes


Mean Goals1.470team-match rows


Goal Variance2.640compare with mean


Zero-Goal Share30.7%possible count-model clue


Max Goals31tail check




Show code
if (nrow(goals_frame) > 0 && "y_goals_for" %in% names(goals_frame)) {
  goals_frame |>
    dplyr::count(y_goals_for, name = "rows") |>
    dplyr::mutate(
      share = fmt_percent(rows / sum(rows), 1),
      rows = fmt_integer(rows)
    ) |>
    display_table(caption = "Observed team-goal distribution.", max_rows = 12)
} else {
  display_table(empty_note("Goal distribution is generated locally from the modeling frame."))
}

Show code
if (nrow(goals_frame) > 0 && "y_goals_for" %in% names(goals_frame) && requireNamespace("ggplot2", quietly = TRUE)) {
  lambda_hat <- mean(goals_frame$y_goals_for, na.rm = TRUE)
  observed <- goals_frame |>
    dplyr::count(y_goals_for, name = "observed_rows") |>
    dplyr::mutate(observed_share = observed_rows / sum(observed_rows))
  poisson_ref <- data.frame(
    y_goals_for = seq(0, max(goals_frame$y_goals_for, na.rm = TRUE)),
    poisson_share = stats::dpois(seq(0, max(goals_frame$y_goals_for, na.rm = TRUE)), lambda = lambda_hat)
  )

  ggplot2::ggplot(observed, ggplot2::aes(x = y_goals_for, y = observed_share)) +
    ggplot2::geom_col(fill = "#157878", alpha = 0.82, width = 0.75) +
    ggplot2::geom_line(data = poisson_ref, ggplot2::aes(y = poisson_share), color = "#f2a65a", linewidth = 1.1) +
    ggplot2::geom_point(data = poisson_ref, ggplot2::aes(y = poisson_share), color = "#f2a65a", size = 2) +
    ggplot2::scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format(accuracy = 1)) +
    ggplot2::labs(
      title = "Observed goals versus simple Poisson reference",
      x = "Goals scored by team",
      y = "Share of team-match rows"
    ) +
    ggplot2::theme_minimal(base_size = 12)
} else {
  plot.new()
  text(0.5, 0.5, "Goal distribution plot is generated locally.")
}





Observed team-goal counts with a Poisson reference curve using the empirical mean.

title: Distributions
text:
3 Candidate Distributions


Goals scoredPoisson, negative binomial, zero-inflated count models, bivariate Poisson, Skellam goal difference.Goals are low nonnegative counts.


Match resultOrdinal logistic, multinomial logistic, adjacent-category models, paired-score simulation.Useful for win/draw/loss odds comparison.


Player and news countsPoisson or negative binomial with exposure offsets where needed.Useful for goals, mentions, injuries, cards, and appearances.


ProbabilitiesBeta, Dirichlet, calibration curves, Brier/log-loss summaries.Useful once predictions are compared with market-implied probabilities.


Continuous contextNormal, t, lognormal, gamma, empirical splines, or transformed variables.Useful for weather, travel, rest, Elo differences, and player minutes.


Latent team strengthGaussian random effects, dynamic state-space ratings, hierarchical priors.Useful when teams have uneven schedule strength and sparse recent games.




Show code
candidate_models <- data.frame(
  data_family = c(
    "Team goals",
    "Goal difference",
    "Win/draw/loss",
    "News article counts",
    "Player availability",
    "Market probabilities",
    "Weather and travel"
  ),
  statistical_shape = c(
    "Low nonnegative count",
    "Integer contrast",
    "Ordered or nominal category",
    "Sparse count",
    "Binary or proportion",
    "Compositional probabilities",
    "Continuous context"
  ),
  model_options = c(
    "Poisson, negative binomial, bivariate Poisson",
    "Skellam, ordered result model",
    "Ordinal logistic, multinomial logistic",
    "Poisson, negative binomial, topic indicators",
    "Logistic regression, hierarchical availability prior",
    "Calibration, beta/Dirichlet summaries, log loss",
    "Splines, t errors, transformed linear effects"
  ),
  diagnostic = c(
    "Mean-variance check, zero share, tail fit",
    "Symmetry and draw concentration",
    "Class balance, calibration by class",
    "Overdispersion and burstiness",
    "Missingness and source reliability",
    "Reliability curves and bookmaker margin",
    "Nonlinearity, skew, outliers"
  )
)

display_table(candidate_models, caption = "Distribution options for the model.")

title: Distributions
text:
4 Review Notes Captured
The public materials reviewed emphasize a few habits that fit this project well:

Use computing to build intuition, not just final answers.
Put distribution assumptions beside visuals so model choices are auditable.
Compare models on held-out or cross-validated performance, not training fit alone.
Teach the reader the data grain before showing the model.
Keep source code reproducible while making the public page readable.

Sources reviewed include Professor Justin Post’s personal/course site, his ST 558 course repository, his Shiny teaching app page, and the Distributome project linked from those teaching resources.
Reference links:

Justin B. Post personal site
ST 558 Data Science for Statisticians
R Shiny teaching apps
Distributome

title: Distributions
text:
5 Model Quality Checklist

Goals distribution diagnostics by tournament era and confederation.
Mean-versus-variance checks for count features.
Calibration plots for ordinal result probabilities.
Cross-validation by time split.
Predicted versus observed W/D/L rates by probability bucket.

title: Goals Model
text:
This report documents the goals model for the World Cup prediction project.
The target is team goals scored in a historical international match:
\[
Y_{i,m} = \text{goals scored by team } i \text{ in match } m
\]
Goals are non-negative counts. The distribution report compares count-model shapes used for soccer scoring.

title: Goals Model
text:
This report documents the goals model for the World Cup prediction project.
The target is team goals scored in a historical international match:
\[
Y_{i,m} = \text{goals scored by team } i \text{ in match } m
\]
Goals are non-negative counts. The distribution report compares count-model shapes used for soccer scoring.

title: Goals Model
text:
2 Unit Of Observation
The modeling frame has one row per team-match.
If Argentina plays France, the data has two rows:

Argentina’s goals as the target, with France as the opponent.
France’s goals as the target, with Argentina as the opponent.

title: Goals Model
text:
3 Sample Rows Embedded In Report
No sample CSV is published with the website. These rows are embedded into the rendered HTML from the local model frame.


Show code
sample_data <- read_csv_if_exists(file.path(root, "data", "processed", "modeling", "goals_linear_model_sample_1000.csv"))

if (nrow(sample_data) > 0) {
  sample_data |>
    dplyr::select(
      date,
      team,
      opponent,
      tournament,
      y_goals_for,
      pre_elo,
      opponent_pre_elo,
      pre_match_expected_result
    ) |>
    dplyr::mutate(
      pre_elo = round(pre_elo, 0),
      opponent_pre_elo = round(opponent_pre_elo, 0),
      pre_match_expected_result = fmt_percent(pre_match_expected_result, 1)
    ) |>
    display_table(caption = "Team-match sample used by the goals model.", max_rows = 10)
} else {
  display_table(empty_note("Sample rows are generated locally by R/10_fit_linear_goals_model.R."))
}

title: Goals Model
text:
4 Elo Feature
For one team against one opponent, the expected result before kickoff is:
\[
p =
\frac{1}{1 + 10^{-\left(\text{Team Elo} + \text{Home Adv.} - \text{Opponent Elo}\right)/400}}
\]
Where:

Team Elo is the team’s pre-match rating.
Opponent Elo is the opponent’s pre-match rating.
Home Adv. is the home advantage adjustment.
p is the expected result on a 0 to 1 scale.

title: Goals Model
text:
7 Interpretation
The signs match soccer intuition:

pre_elo is positive: stronger teams score more.
opponent_pre_elo is negative: stronger opponents suppress scoring.
pre_match_expected_result is positive: teams expected to perform better score more.
listed_homeTRUE is positive: listed home teams score more.

8 Reproduce Locally

title: 2026 World Cup Betting Model
text:
DRAFT
This site presents the 2026 World Cup betting model, the data used to support it, and the model results in one shareable place.
Code is hidden by default so the report reads cleanly. Use Show code to inspect the R steps.
No raw datasets, processed databases, API keys, .Renviron, credentials, or private files are published here.

title: 2026 World Cup Betting Model
text:
1 Data Sources and Expense Overview

Primary live source: API-Football by API-Sports
The model uses API-Football for World Cup fixtures, teams, standings, player/squad data, injuries, lineups, match events, player statistics, odds, and predictions where coverage is available.




Data sources used by the modeling workflow.


Layer
Source
Use in Model




Live football API
API-Football / API-Sports
Fixtures, teams, standings, injuries, lineups, odds, and match details


Historical match results
Public international results and goalscorer datasets
Team form, goals scored, results, Elo-style strength features


Player and squad context
API-Football plus public squad references
Roster strength, player goal history, availability indicators


Venue, weather, and news context
Open venue/weather/news sources
Travel, venue, weather, and news-derived context features


Publishing
GitHub Pages
Shareable HTML reports and model summaries





The public site is hosted without an additional hosting bill. The live football data subscription is the main recurring project expense, and the project is kept within the monthly budget cap.

title: 2026 World Cup Betting Model
text:
2 Model Iterations
The model section is organized around interpretable iterations. Each iteration names the prediction target, shows the equation, explains the coefficients in plain English, and lists the checks that determine whether the model is trustworthy.


2.1 1. Simple OLS Goals Model
Question: how many goals should a team score in one match?
The simple goals model starts with team strength difference:
\[
\text{Goals} = \beta_0 + \beta_1\text{Strength Difference} + \epsilon
\]
In words, beta_1 is the average change in expected goals for a one-unit increase in strength difference.
Why start here: OLS is easy to explain, fast to diagnose, and gives a clear benchmark before count models.
Main issues to check:

Goals are counts, so OLS can predict impossible values below 0.
Soccer scores are low and skewed, so residuals may not look normal.
Error variance can change by team strength or opponent strength.
Team-match rows are not fully independent because each match creates two rows.
Missing roster, injury, rest, venue, and market data can bias coefficients.



2.2 2. Three-Outcome Result Model
Question: what is the probability of win, draw, or loss?
The first three-outcome model is an ordinal logistic regression because the team result has a natural order:
\[
\text{loss} < \text{draw} < \text{win}
\]
The model uses cumulative logits:
\[
\text{logit}\{P(\text{Result} \leq k)\}
= \theta_k - \eta
\]
with:
\[
\eta = \beta_1\text{Strength Difference}
+ \beta_2\text{Roster Strength}
+ \beta_3\text{Rest}
+ \beta_4\text{Venue}
\]
The output is three probabilities:
\[
P(\text{loss}), \quad P(\text{draw}), \quad P(\text{win})
\]
Main issues to check:

The proportional-odds assumption may be too strong.
Draws are not simply halfway between loss and win.
A multinomial logit may fit better if win, draw, and loss behave as separate categories.
Calibration matters more than raw accuracy for betting comparison.
Market odds should be the benchmark once API odds are available.

title: 2026 World Cup Betting Model
text:
3 Dictionary
Use this as the quick reference for acronyms and modeling terms that appear across the site.








Term
Plain-English Meaning
Why It Matters Here




Y or target
The thing the model is trying to predict.
For the goals model, Y is goals scored. For the result model, Y is win, draw, or loss.


X or predictor
An input used to make the prediction.
Team strength, opponent strength, rest, venue, injuries, and odds can all be predictors.


Regression
A model that estimates how a target changes with predictors.
It gives a structured way to turn soccer information into expected goals or probabilities.


OLS
Ordinary Least Squares. A line-fitting method that chooses coefficients by minimizing squared prediction errors.
It is the first goals benchmark because it is easy to explain and diagnose.


RMSE
Root Mean Squared Error. A typical prediction miss, measured in the same units as the target.
If goals RMSE is 1.46, the model is often off by roughly 1 to 2 goals, with big misses punished more. Lower is better.


Coefficient
The estimated effect of one predictor, holding the other predictors fixed.
A positive strength coefficient means stronger teams are expected to score more, all else equal.


Residual
Observed value minus predicted value.
Residual plots show whether the model is missing patterns.


Logit
A transformation that converts a probability into log odds.
Logistic models use logits so probabilities stay between 0 and 1.


Odds
Probability of an event divided by probability of it not happening.
Model probabilities can be compared with betting-market odds after converting both to the same scale.


Ordinal logistic regression
A logistic model for ordered outcomes.
It fits loss < draw < win while producing probabilities for all three outcomes.


Multinomial logistic regression
A logistic model for three or more categories without assuming an order.
It is the backup if draws behave like their own special category rather than the middle of loss and win.


Accuracy
Share of predictions where the top predicted class matches the actual result.
Useful for a quick check, but not enough for betting because probability quality matters too.


Calibration
Whether predicted probabilities match real frequencies.
If the model says 60% win chance, well-calibrated teams should win about 60% of those cases.


Log loss
A probability score that penalizes confident wrong predictions.
Better than accuracy when the model outputs win/draw/loss probabilities. Lower is better.


Brier score
Average squared error between predicted probabilities and actual outcomes.
Another way to judge probability quality. Lower is better.


Test set
Data held out from fitting and used only to evaluate performance.
It helps estimate how the model performs on games it has not already seen.


Overfitting
When a model learns noise in past data instead of repeatable signal.
A model can look good historically and still fail on new World Cup matches.


Data grain
What one row means in a dataset.
The main modeling grain is one team in one match. One match creates two team-match rows.


API
Application Programming Interface. A structured way to pull data from a provider.
API-Football is the primary live football data source.

title: 2026 World Cup Betting Model
text:
4 Project Map

AboutData ExplorationModelingDataDisplay Standards


This project builds a reproducible data and modeling workflow for the 2026 FIFA World Cup.
The workflow has four layers:

Data collection: match history, Elo ratings, 2026 venues, weather, player metadata, odds, and news signals.
Data storage: local files and DuckDB tables for modeling.
Modeling: interpretable models in R, with Python handling source refreshes and heavier data jobs.
Publishing: Quarto renders a shareable GitHub Pages site from the local analysis.



The starting modeling unit is one row per team-match. A single game creates two observations, one for each team.


Show code
goals_sample <- read_csv_if_exists(file.path(root, "data", "processed", "modeling", "goals_linear_model_sample_1000.csv"))

if (nrow(goals_sample) > 0) {
  goals_sample |>
    dplyr::select(
      date,
      team,
      opponent,
      tournament,
      y_goals_for,
      pre_elo,
      opponent_pre_elo,
      pre_match_expected_result
    ) |>
    dplyr::mutate(
      pre_match_expected_result = fmt_percent(pre_match_expected_result, 1),
      pre_elo = round(pre_elo, 0),
      opponent_pre_elo = round(opponent_pre_elo, 0)
    ) |>
    display_table(caption = "Sample of the team-match modeling grain.", max_rows = 8)
} else {
  display_table(empty_note("Sample rows are generated locally by R/10_fit_linear_goals_model.R."))
}

    ggplot2::geom_histogram(binwidth = 1, boundary = -0.5, fill = "#157878", color = "white") +

      y = "Team-match rows"

    ggplot2::theme_minimal(base_size = 12)
} else {
  plot.new()
  text(0.5, 0.5, "Goals sample is generated locally before rendering.")
}





Distribution of goals in the public sample used for the goals model.






The model suite has two core outputs:

Goals OLS: predicts goals scored.
Ordinal logistic: predicts loss < draw < win.

The reports show the model target, data grain, math, coefficients, predictions, and diagnostics.


Show code
if (nrow(goals_metrics) > 0 && nrow(ordinal_metrics) > 0) {
  data.frame(
    model = c("Goals OLS", "Ordinal result logistic"),
    target = c("team goals", "loss < draw < win"),
    rows = fmt_integer(c(goals_metrics$rows_total[[1]], ordinal_metrics$rows_total[[1]])),
    primary_metric = c("Test RMSE", "Test accuracy"),
    value = c(
      fmt_number(goals_metrics$test_rmse[[1]], 3),
      fmt_percent(ordinal_metrics$test_accuracy[[1]], 1)
    ),
    context = c(
      paste("Mean test goals:", fmt_number(goals_metrics$mean_y_test[[1]], 3)),
      paste("Reference accuracy:", fmt_percent(ordinal_metrics$baseline_majority_accuracy[[1]], 1))
    )
  ) |>
    display_table(caption = "Current model performance.")
} else {
  display_table(empty_note("Model metrics are generated locally before rendering."))
}

The modeling database stores raw and transformed tables. The rendered site publishes metadata and model outputs, while credentials and private source files stay local.

One grain per tableRows should mean one thing: team-match, match, player, venue, or API pull.


Readable labelsKeep machine-safe names in source files; convert them to readable labels only in reports.


Round for display onlyDo not round model inputs or stored outputs. Round the rendered table so readers can scan it.


Separate public and privatePublish metadata and rendered results, not raw datasets, databases, keys, or credentials.

title: 2026 World Cup Betting Model
text:
5 Reports



5.1 Goals Model
OLS model for team goals scored from pre-match Elo, opponent Elo, expected result, home listing, and neutral-site status.
Open goals report



5.2 Result Model
Ordinal logistic model for loss < draw < win, producing win/draw/loss probability estimates.
Open result report




5.3 Distribution Review
Distribution notes for deciding whether soccer features are counts, continuous variables, ordered outcomes, probabilities, or mixture processes.
Open distribution review

title: Result Probability Model
text:
This report documents the result-probability model.
The ordered outcome is:
\[
Y_{i,m}^{ord} \in \{\text{loss}, \text{draw}, \text{win}\}
\]
with:
\[
\text{loss} < \text{draw} < \text{win}
\]
This output supports betting-market comparison because it estimates match outcome probabilities.

title: Result Probability Model
text:
This report documents the result-probability model.
The ordered outcome is:
\[
Y_{i,m}^{ord} \in \{\text{loss}, \text{draw}, \text{win}\}
\]
with:
\[
\text{loss} < \text{draw} < \text{win}
\]
This output supports betting-market comparison because it estimates match outcome probabilities.

title: Result Probability Model
text:
2 Unit Of Observation
The data has one row per team-match.
If Argentina plays France, there are two rows:

Argentina result versus France.
France result versus Argentina.

title: Result Probability Model
text:
3 Embedded Sample Rows
No sample CSV is published with the website. These rows are embedded into the rendered HTML from the local model frame.


Show code
sample_data <- read_csv_if_exists(file.path(root, "data", "processed", "modeling", "result_ordinal_model_sample_1000.csv"))

if (nrow(sample_data) > 0) {
  sample_data |>
    dplyr::select(
      date,
      team,
      opponent,
      y_result_ordered,
      pre_elo,
      opponent_pre_elo,
      pre_match_expected_result
    ) |>
    dplyr::mutate(
      pre_elo = round(pre_elo, 0),
      opponent_pre_elo = round(opponent_pre_elo, 0),
      pre_match_expected_result = fmt_percent(pre_match_expected_result, 1)
    ) |>
    display_table(caption = "Team-match sample used by the result model.", max_rows = 10)
} else {
  display_table(empty_note("Sample rows are generated locally by R/11_fit_ordinal_result_model.R."))
}

title: Result Probability Model
Result Probability Model
text:
5 Results


Show code
metrics <- read_csv_if_exists(file.path(root, "data", "processed", "modeling", "result_ordinal_model_metrics.csv"))
class_distribution <- read_csv_if_exists(file.path(root, "data", "processed", "modeling", "result_ordinal_model_class_distribution.csv"))
coefficients <- read_csv_if_exists(file.path(root, "data", "processed", "modeling", "result_ordinal_model_coefficients.csv"))
confusion <- read_csv_if_exists(file.path(root, "data", "processed", "modeling", "result_ordinal_model_confusion_matrix.csv"))
predictions <- read_csv_if_exists(file.path(root, "data", "processed", "modeling", "result_ordinal_model_test_predictions.csv"))

if (nrow(metrics) > 0) {
  data.frame(
    rows_total = fmt_integer(metrics$rows_total),
    rows_train = fmt_integer(metrics$rows_train),
    rows_test = fmt_integer(metrics$rows_test),
    test_accuracy = fmt_percent(metrics$test_accuracy, 1),
    reference_accuracy = fmt_percent(metrics$baseline_majority_accuracy, 1),
    test_log_loss = fmt_number(metrics$test_log_loss, 3),
    test_multiclass_brier = fmt_number(metrics$test_multiclass_brier, 3)
  ) |>
    display_table(caption = "Ordinal model fit summary.")
} else {
  display_table(empty_note("Metrics are generated locally."))
}

title: Result Probability Model

7 Coefficients


Show code
if (nrow(coefficients) > 0) {
  coefficients |>
    dplyr::mutate(
      estimate = fmt_number(estimate, 4),
      std_error = fmt_number(std_error, 4),
      t_value = fmt_number(t_value, 2),
      p_value_approx = fmt_p_value(p_value_approx)
    ) |>
    display_table(caption = "Ordinal logistic coefficients and thresholds.")
} else {
  display_table(empty_note("Coefficients are generated locally."))
}

title: Result Probability Model
text:
8 Test Prediction Examples


Show code
if (nrow(predictions) > 0) {
  predictions |>
    dplyr::select(
      date,
      team,
      opponent,
      y_result_ordered,
      predicted_result,
      pred_prob_loss,
      pred_prob_draw,
      pred_prob_win
    ) |>
    dplyr::mutate(
      pred_prob_loss = fmt_percent(pred_prob_loss, 1),
      pred_prob_draw = fmt_percent(pred_prob_draw, 1),
      pred_prob_win = fmt_percent(pred_prob_win, 1)
    ) |>
    display_table(caption = "Example test-set predictions.", max_rows = 12)
} else {
  display_table(empty_note("Predictions are generated locally."))
}

title: Result Probability Model
text:
9 Confusion Matrix


Show code
if (nrow(confusion) > 0) {
  confusion |>
    dplyr::mutate(rows = fmt_integer(rows)) |>
    display_table(caption = "Confusion matrix counts by actual and predicted result.")
} else {
  display_table(empty_note("Confusion matrix is generated locally."))
}



Confusion matrix counts by actual and predicted result.

Show code
if (nrow(confusion) > 0 && requireNamespace("ggplot2", quietly = TRUE)) {
  confusion |>
    ggplot2::ggplot(ggplot2::aes(x = predicted_result, y = actual_result, fill = rows)) +
    ggplot2::geom_tile(color = "white", linewidth = 0.8) +
    ggplot2::geom_text(ggplot2::aes(label = format(rows, big.mark = ",")), color = "white", fontface = "bold") +
    ggplot2::scale_fill_gradient(low = "#9cc7c7", high = "#157878") +
    ggplot2::labs(
      title = "Ordinal result model confusion matrix",
      x = "Predicted result",
      y = "Actual result",
      fill = "Rows"
    ) +
    ggplot2::theme_minimal(base_size = 12)
} else {
  plot.new()
  text(0.5, 0.5, "Confusion matrix plot is generated locally.")
}





Confusion matrix as a heatmap; darker cells have more team-match rows.

title: Result Probability Model
text:
10 Interpretation
The model produces:
\[
P(\text{loss}), \quad P(\text{draw}), \quad P(\text{win})
\]
These probabilities are the shape used for odds comparison.
Model checks tracked here:

Proportional-odds fit.
Paired team-match dependence.
Draw calibration.
Comparison against market-implied probabilities.

title: Result Probability Model
text:
11 Reproduce Locally


Show code
source("R/01_build_duckdb.R")
source("R/11_fit_ordinal_result_model.R")
